coredata.dc:title: Efficient UV-shielding coatings with carbon quantum dots
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Ponte, Clara
Grosche, Lucas C.
Kolen'ko, Yury V.
Sousa, Juliana P.S.
coredata.dc:description: Most polymers are susceptible to ultraviolet (UV) radiation, showing irreversible damage after extended exposures, which consequently affects the durability of paints. The currently used inorganic UV protective compounds struggle to remediate this problem due to their short UV absorption range and poor photostability, resulting in the deterioration of the polymeric coatings. Recently, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have been raising great interest owing to their excellent UV absorption and photostability with high compatibility in different polymeric systems, making them promising UV absorbers. The present work reports the synthesis of nitrogen-doped CQDs and their characterization by well-established techniques regarding their chemical composition, morphology, size, thermal stability, and optical properties. Different amounts of CQDs were incorporated in a commercial polyurethane (PU)-based matrix. The UV-shielding capacity, and chemical and mechanical properties of the developed coatings were evaluated. The present study reports the preparation of 40 μm thick CQDs@PU coatings with a high UV-shielding capacity of up to 98% under direct sunlight. Also, the prepared coatings were revealed to be resistant to abrasion and have good washability and adhesion to the metallic substrate.
coredata.openaccessSponsorName: Portugal institutions: Core Hybrid journals RAP 2025
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
UV protection
Coatings

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the synthesis procedure for CQDs and their incorporation into the polymeric formulation.
FTIR spectrum of the as-synthesized N-doped CQDs.
A survey XPS spectrum (a) and high-resolution XPS data for the C1s (b), O1s (c) and N1s (d) regions collected from the as-synthesized CQDs.
Representative low-magnification TEM image of the synthesized CQDs (left panel), together with the estimated particle size distribution histogram (right panel).
TGA profile and the respective derivative curve for the synthesized CQDs.
Collected UV–Vis absorption spectrum of an aqueous dispersion of the synthesized CQDs, diluted 1: 100.
Full UV–Vis range (a) and close-up UV region (b) transmittance spectra recorded for the 40 μm-thick PU coatings with various mass loadings of the synthesized CQDs.
UV-shielding capability of the fabricated 40 μm-thick PU coatings as a function of the CQDs' concentration under UV lamp light (a) and natural sunlight (b).
Digital photos of the 40 μm-thick PU coatings before and after the abrasion test: (a) control bare PU film and (b) PU film with 30 mg/gPU CDQs.
Digital photos of the 40 μm-thick PU coatings before (a, b) and after (c, d) adhesion test: (a, c) PU film with 30 mg/gPU CDQs (b, d) and control bare PU film.

Digital photos of the 40 μm-thick PU coatings after the application of the chemical (a, d), drying for 18 h at the ambient conditions (b, e), and washing (c, f) for control bare PU film (a–c) and PU film with 30 mg/gPU CDQs (d–f). 1 – baby oil, 2 – cooking oil, 3 – orange juice without gas, 4 – wine, 5 – vinegar, 6 – lipstick, 7 – mustard, 8 – ketchup, and 9 – espresso coffee with sugar.
Average values of the mass losses measured after the abrasion testing of control and CQDs-containing 40 μm-thick PU coatings (average ± standard deviation, n = 2).

head.ce:title: Efficient UV-shielding coatings with carbon quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: 4iTEC - Smart Solutions for Manufacturing, Lugar do Pombal, Zona Industrial do Salgueiro, 3530-259 Mangualde, Portugal

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Lugar do Pombal, Zona Industrial do Salgueiro

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: 4iTEC - Smart Solutions for Manufacturing, Lugar do Pombal, Zona Industrial do Salgueiro, 3530-259 Mangualde, Portugal

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Most polymers are susceptible to ultraviolet (UV) radiation, showing irreversible damage after extended exposures, which consequently affects the durability of paints. The currently used inorganic UV protective compounds struggle to remediate this problem due to their short UV absorption range and poor photostability, resulting in the deterioration of the polymeric coatings. Recently, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have been raising great interest owing to their excellent UV absorption and photostability with high compatibility in different polymeric systems, making them promising UV absorbers.

The present work reports the synthesis of nitrogen-doped CQDs and their characterization by well-established techniques regarding their chemical composition, morphology, size, thermal stability, and optical properties. Different amounts of CQDs were incorporated in a commercial polyurethane (PU)-based matrix. The UV-shielding capacity, and chemical and mechanical properties of the developed coatings were evaluated. The present study reports the preparation of 40 μm thick CQDs@PU coatings with a high UV-shielding capacity of up to 98% under direct sunlight. Also, the prepared coatings were revealed to be resistant to abrasion and have good washability and adhesion to the metallic substrate.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Ultraviolet (UV) irradiation is divided into three regions depending on the wavelength, namely, UVA (315–400 nm), UVB (315–280 nm), and UVC (280–200 nm). Since only UVA and UVB can reach the Earth's surface, they are the most damaging irradiations for humankind, especially high-energy UVB one [1–3]. Several reports have proven that long exposure to UV light could severely affect not only human health (e.g., skin and eyes) but also drastically reduce the durability of dyes, pigments, polymers, plastics, woods, papers, and other materials [2–5]. Hence, many efforts have been devoted to protecting such types of soft mater materials against photodegradation under UV irradiation, thus, improving their durability during outdoor usage.

Recently, efforts have been initiated to develop advanced coatings with UV-shielding properties. In industry, a common strategy to combat UV-induced photodegradation involves protecting photodegradable materials by applying coatings embedded with UV-absorbing agents. These agents typically convert the absorbed UV radiation into less harmful thermal energy or visible light. UV absorbers can generally be categorized into two groups: organic compounds (e.g., avobenzone, benzophenone, octocrylene, benzotriazole, salicylic acid) and inorganic compounds (e.g., titanium dioxide, zinc oxide, cerium dioxide). Organic compounds, while effective UV absorbers, often suffer from low photostability and may degrade into toxic, bioaccumulative byproducts [6,7]. As a result, inorganic UV-absorbing agents are frequently preferred. However, many inorganic compounds, such as the aforementioned metal oxides, exhibit photocatalytic activity after prolonged UV exposure. This activity can accelerate the photodegradation of protective polymeric coatings or paints, leading to the loss of their mechanical integrity [2,7,8].To address these challenges, we focused on developing novel, photostable UV-absorbing agents for more effective UV-protective coatings. Given the strong UV absorption properties of carbon quantum dots (CQDs), we explored this emerging class of quantum materials as potential candidates for UV-shielding applications [9].

CQDs are luminescent carbon nanoparticles with lateral sizes below tens of nanometers [10]. Since their introduction by Xu and co-workers in 2004 [11], CQDs have gained a great deal of attention in various fields of research, e.g., electronics, optics, catalysis, energy, sensing, biomedicine, and others [12–14]. In the coating science, several unique features make CQDs an excellent candidate for UV-shielding applications against photodegradation of the polymeric coatings/paints. Specifically, CQDs have been shown to exhibit interesting physicochemical properties, such as excellent and tuneable photoluminescence, high quantum yield, small size, tuneable bandgap, low toxicity, robust synthesis from abundant resources, feasibility for elemental doping, as well as broad and strong absorption of UV irradiation, plus superior photostability even under prolonged exposure to UV light [15–20]. In addition, CQDs are characterized by an excellent aqueous dispersibility that provides easy access to their homogeneous dispersion within different polymeric paint formulations [21] and, accordingly, allows the production of uniform UV-absorbing polymeric thin films [22].

This study developed coatings based on nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (CQDs) with UV-shielding properties, designed to function as protective layers for automotive paintwork. The synthesis and characterization of nitrogen-doped CQDs were conducted, and these CQDs were incorporated into polyurethane (PU) formulations at various concentrations. Polyurethane was selected due to its widespread use in the automotive industry, ensuring the practical relevance of the developed coatings. The synthesized CQDs were integrated into PU formulations at different concentrations, and the UV-shielding performance of the resulting 40-μm-thick CQD-containing PU coatings was evaluated using two methods: (i) UV–vis spectroscopy with an integrating sphere and (ii) a digital UV meter under irradiation from two light sources, a UV lamp and sunlight. Furthermore, the chemical and mechanical properties of the CQD-containing PU coatings were thoroughly analysed. The results demonstrated that the CQD-based coatings achieved up to 98% UV-shielding under direct sunlight, identifying an optimal CQD concentration for maximizing coating performance. These findings underscore the potential of CQDs as efficient UV absorbers for automotive applications.

Citric acid (CA, ≥ 99.5%), poly(vinyl alcohol) (PVA, M w = 13,000–23,000 hydrolysed, 98%), polyethyleneimine branched (b-PEI, M w ≈ 25,000, M n ≈ 10,000, ≥ 99%) and 1-methoxy-2-propyl acetate (≥99.5%) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. Xylene (≥ 99%) was acquired from Fisher Chemical. To prepare the two-component polyurethane (PU) formulation, the acrylic polyol (SETALUX® D A665 BA/X, Allnex Resins), the aliphatic polyisocyanate (Desmodur® ultra N 3390 BA, Covestro), and the additives (BYK-331, DISPERBYK-161, BYK) were used.

CQDs were synthesized by the hydrothermal method [23]. Briefly, in a Teflon container, 10% of PVA, 10% of CA and 2% of b-PEI were dissolved in 25 mL of deionized water under magnetic stirring at room temperature. Then, the Teflon liner was placed into a stainless-steel autoclave, sealed, and kept at 180 °C for 4 h. After the hydrothermal synthesis, the CQDs were purified using dialysis membranes (0.1–0.5 kD, v/L = 3,1 mL/cm) and then recovered after 24 h. An aqueous CQDs dispersion was prepared with a concentration of 31 mg/mL.

ce:section.ce:section-title: General Procedure for CQDs-containing PU coatings
ce:section.ce:para: CQDs-containing polyurethane films were prepared by dispersing different amounts of the as-synthesized CQDs (0.5–30 mg/gPU − 1) in a two-component PU matrix. First, a calculated amount of CQDs' aqueous solution was dried at 80 °C, redispersed in 500 μL of deionized water, and mixed with Component A (mixture of SETALUX® D A 665 BA/X, BYK-331 and 1-methoxy 2-propyl acetate/xylene (1:1) of the polyurethane matrix under magnetic stirring. Then, the Component B (Desmodur® ultra N 3390 BA and DISPERBYK-161) was added and mixed using a laboratory homogenizer (Homogenizer M from VWRTM). Thereafter, the mixtures were applied to the substrate using an automatic bar coater (K control coater from RK print coat instruments), maintaining a constant film thickness of 40 μm. The as-prepared coating was left for 15 min at room temperature and then subjected to the curing process at 140 °C for 25 min. This procedure was performed in stainless steel AISI 304 2B cold laminated plates with 6.0 × 2.5 × 0.1 cm (L × W × H) and glass substrates with 7.6 × 2.6 × 0.1 cm (L × W × H). All substrates were previously cleaned first with detergent and a soft sponge to remove grease traces, then deionized water and isopropanol were applied. Fig. 1 presents a schematic illustration of the synthesis procedure for CQDs and their incorporation into the polymeric formulation.

Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) analysis was performed on a VERTEX 80v FT-IR spectrometer (Bruker) in attenuated total reflectance (ATR) mode. The FTIR spectra were collected in the 400–4000 cm−1 range using a resolution of 4 cm−1 and 64 scans. The FTIR data is background corrected and reported in frequency of absorption (cm−1).

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed on an ultra-high vacuum (UHV) ESCALAB 250Xi system (Thermo Fisher Scientific) using a hemispherical analyser and monochromatic Al K α radiation (hν = 1486.7 eV), operated at 15 keV and 200 W power. The analysis chamber was always maintained under a vacuum less than 5× 10−8 Pa. The values of binding energies (BE) were calibrated concerning the C1s peak at 284.0 eV, referred to as the sp2 carbon. XPS data were peak fitted through Avantage data processing software (Thermo Fisher Scientific). The fitting function was a Gauss-Lorentz mix, and the background was subtracted using the Shirley-type method.

To investigate the size and morphology of the synthesized CQDs, transmission electron microscopy (TEM) analysis was carried out using a double-corrected Titan Themis electron microscope (Thermo Fisher Scientific). The particle size distribution of the CQDs was estimated using the ImageJ software package.
The thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) was performed using a TGA/DSC 1 STARe system (Mettler Toledo). The powdered CQDs were heated from 30 to 900 °C at 5 °C/min under a continuous argon flow.

The ultraviolet-visible spectroscopy (UV–Vis) analysis of the CQDs' aqueous dispersions was performed using a UV-2550 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu), collected in the wavelength range 280–800 nm with a resolution of 1 nm.
The UV–Vis transmittance spectra of the CQDs-containing PU coatings were collected in a LAMBDA 950 UV-VIS-NIR spectrophotometer (PerkinElmer) using a 60 mm collection integrating sphere module.

The UV light intensity of the light sources, glass substrates, bare polymer, and prepared CQDs-containing PU coatings was measured with a UV-340 A UV digital meter (Lutron). Two different light sources, namely, a UV lamp (125 W) and direct daylight, were used to investigate the UV-shielding properties of the resultant CQDs-containing PU coatings.

Among the various reported methods for synthesizing carbon quantum dots (CQDs), hydrothermal synthesis has proven to be one of the most effective approaches [13,14]. Following this method and adapting the synthesis and purification protocol described by Hess and colleagues [23], nitrogen-doped CQDs were successfully produced and stored as an aqueous dispersion.

To investigate the chemical structure of the as-synthesized CQDs, FTIR analysis was conducted (Fig. 2 ), and the obtained results indicate the presence of several functional groups in the CQDs. Specifically, the broad band centred at 3284 cm−1 is attributed to the stretching vibrations of the OH and NH bonds, suggesting the presence of the characteristic groups from the precursor citric acid and PEI polymer. The vibrational bands at 2938 and 2852 cm−1 are characteristic of the respective asymmetric and symmetric stretching modes of the CH bonds from the alkyl groups. The absorption band at 1700 cm−1 is ascribed to the stretching vibration mode of the CO bonds, while the peaks at 1648 and 1554 cm−1 are related to the bending vibration modes of the NH bonds. The strong and sharp vibrational band at 1084 cm−1 is attributed to the stretching vibration modes of the CO bonds [5,23,24].

To gain insight into the surface chemical composition of the as-synthesized CQDs, the XPS analysis was carried out. Fig. 3 a, shows a survey of XPS spectra where one could observe three main peaks at binding energies (BE) 284.4, 399.4, and 531.4 eV that correspond to C1s (70.7%), N1s (4.8%), and O1s (24.5%), respectively. The C1s peak (Fig. 3b) was resolved into three components centred at BE 284.1, 285.4, and 288.0 eV, which are assigned to CC, CN, and OCO bonds, respectively. The high-resolution spectrum of O1s (Fig. 3c) revealed two peaks centred at BE 531.4 and 532.6 eV, which are associated with the presence of the CO and COH / COC groups. The fitting of the high-resolution N1s spectrum reveals the presence of pyridinic (399.7 eV, 93.8%), pyrrolic (401.3 eV, 5.5%), and graphitic (402.1 eV, 0.7%) nitrogen atoms. These results confirm that the as-synthesized CQDs are nitrogen-doped [5,25,26], in good agreement with the FTIR analysis (Fig. 2).

After probing the chemical structure and composition of the as-synthesized CQDs, the morphology and particle size distribution (PSD) were explored by transmission electron microscopy. Fig. 4 , shows a low-magnification TEM image of the resultant CQDs, together with the estimated PSD histogram. The hydrothermal synthesis product was observed to consist of well-dispersed quasispherical carbon nanoparticles with an average diameter of 9 ± 2 nm, consistent with previously reported data [23,24].

Attention was then turned towards the evaluation of the thermal behaviour of the prepared N-doped CQDs using thermogravimetric analysis (Fig. 5 ). The collected TGA profile reveals two main weight losses upon the heating of CQDs from 30 to 900 °C under an inert atmosphere. The first weight loss (≈16%), occurred in the temperature range from 100 to 300 °C, which is related to the volatilization of physically and chemically adsorbed water molecules from the CQDs' surface, as well as to the initial degradation of CQDs' oxygen and nitrogen groups. Then, an abrupt weight loss step is observed (≈67%), most likely due to the further degradation of the functional groups present on the CQDs' surface [27].

The capability of the as-synthesized CQDs to absorb UV irradiation was assessed by UV–Vis spectroscopy, and the results indicate that the CQDs have a significant UV-absorbing behaviour. Fig. 6 shows the absorption spectrum of an aqueous dispersion of CQDs with a concentration of 0.31 mg/mL. The absorption curve reveals an evident peak centred at around 350 nm, which is related to the characteristic n–π* transitions of the nitrogen/oxygen-containing groups at the edge of the CQDs [28]. Despite the strong absorption in the UV range, the absorbance in the visible light range was found to be nearly zero.

Having successfully prepared high-quality nanosized CQDs well-dispersed in water, they were further incorporated into benchmark two-component polyurethane (PU) coating formulation, targeting UV-shielding properties. The formulation was then deposited onto glass and metal substrates with the help of an automatic bar coater (K control coater) followed by a curing process, thus affording reproducible preparation of 40 μm-thick CQDs-containing PU films.

The as-fabricated films were first subjected to the investigation of their optical properties. Fig. 7 displays the UV–Vis transmittance spectra of the prepared PU coatings with different loadings of the CQDs, and the bare PU coating without CQDs. The transmittance values were measured to determine the coatings' effectiveness in blocking UV radiation. Lower transmittance values indicate higher UV-shielding capacities, while higher values suggest reduced UV protection. Notably, bare PU coating demonstrates high transparency for both UV and Vis ranges, indicating that the polymeric PU matrix solo does not exhibit UV-shielding properties. In sharp contrast, an obvious change in the coatings transmittance behaviour, especially in the UV region, was observed after the incorporation of the CQDs (Fig. 7). PU coatings with small loadings of the CQDs (0.5–10.0 mg/gPU) demonstrate an evident decrease, between 340 and 400 nm, followed by a slight increase in the transmittance values. This is a result of the maximum UV absorption of the CQDs centred at 350 nm (Fig. 6). Upon further increase of the loadings of the CQDs to 20 and 30 mg/gPU, the curves become almost a plateau, showing transmittance values of 15 ± 1% and 13 ± 1% in the UVA range, and 20 ± 1% and 17 ± 1% in the UVB range, respectively.

Encouraged by a good optical transmittance of the fabricated 40 μm-thick CQDs-containing PU coatings, their UV-shielding properties were also investigated using a UV light meter. This tool can measure the absolute (calibrated) intensity of UV irradiation within a fixed wavelength range (290–390 nm). The experiments were performed under artificial UV lamp irradiation as well as under natural sunlight, and the obtained results are shown in Fig. 8 .

Notably, the initial UV light intensities from the UV lamp and sunlight were 1405 and 1763 μW/cm2, respectively, and the intensities remained almost unaffected when the control bare PU coating was placed on the UV detector (Fig. 8). In contrast, the detected UV light intensity started to decrease upon the gradual increase of the CQDs concentration in PU coatings (Fig. 8), and, consequently, an increase in the UV-shielding efficiency was observed. Importantly, the fabricated 40 μm-thick CQDs-containing PU films demonstrate very similar behaviours for both UV light sources used (Fig. 8). For instance, the 40 μm-thick PU film with 0.5 mg/gPU CQDs blocks 19% (1135 μW/cm2) and 23% (1360 μW/cm2) of the UV lamp light and sunlight, respectively. At the same time, PU film with 1.0 mg/gPU CQDs shields 26% (1035 μW/cm2 @ UV lamp) and 30% (1230 μW/cm2 @ sunlight) of the irradiation. On the other hand, with just 5 and 10 mg/gPU concentrations of the CQDs, PU coatings could block around 60% (550 μW/cm2 @ UV lamp; 663 μW/cm2 @ sunlight) and 81% (263 μW/cm2 @ UV lamp; 327 μW/cm2 @ sunlight), respectively, of the incoming UV irradiation from both light sources applied. Remarkably, when the loading of CQDs was further increased to 20 and 30 mg/gPU, almost complete shielding of the UV irradiation was observed (Fig. 8), revealing outstanding UV-shielding efficiency values of 96% (62 μW/cm2 @ UV lamp; 68 μW/cm2 @ sunlight) and 98% (41 μW/cm2 @ UV lamp; 18 μW/cm2 @ sunlight), respectively.

After underpinning the excellent UV-shielding properties of the fabricated 40 μm-thick PU coatings containing CQDs, their mechanical and chemical properties were next examined that the incorporation of CQDs into benchmark two-component PU coating does not impact its mechanical and chemical properties but plays a direct role in the development of the aforementioned UV transmittance and absorbance properties.

The data indicates that there were no significant differences in the abrasion resistance between control and CQDs-containing PU coatings, with mass losses of 3.8 and 5.0 mg, respectively, following the abrasion test (Table 1). These values correspond to less than 5 % of the total initial mass, highlighting the minimal impact of abrasion on the coatings' overall integrity. Furthermore, digital photos of the test specimens before and after the abrasion test reveal that both films kept their visual appearance almost intact (Fig. 9), even after 500 cycles. Notably, the grey shade observed after the test, is due to the dragging phenomenon of the metallic part of the substrate that was significantly affected by the abrasive wheels.

The adhesion properties of the fabricated coatings were next assessed following the standard specifications of ASTM D3359–09 – Method B, which consisted of applying and removing a pressure-sensitive tape over pre-made patterned lattice cuts in coatings deposited on metallic substrate. This testing was also performed in duplicate on the control bare PU coating and one PU coating CQDs' concentration of 30 mg/gPU. Fig. 10 shows the coatings before and after adhesion testing, and as one can note, none of the squares of the lattice detaches from the substrate during the tape test for both coatings, and the edges of the cuts remain completely smooth. Thus, the adhesion of the prepared coatings can be classified with a rate of 5B according to the classification table illustrated in the aforementioned standard.

Finally, attention was turned towards investigating the chemical stability of the fabricated coatings. For this purpose, the washability test was conducted according to the routine described in the ASTM D4828–94 standard. Briefly, the test determines the relative ease of removing stains caused by daily use of products. A drop of the product, such as baby oil, cooking oil, vinegar, wine, mustard, ketchup, coffee, juice, and lipstick, was placed on control bare and CDQs-containing 40 μm-thick PU and coatings and let on the surface of the coating for 18 h. Next, the coatings were manually washed with a soft sponge, 5 mL of liquid cleanser, and 15 mL of deionized water for continuous 100 cycles, perpendicular to the surface of the coating. Notably, it was observed during the test that almost all chemical products were easily removed in less than 10 washing cycles, while ketchup was only removed after close to 100 washing cycles. At the same time, mustard was found to remain even after the 100 washing cycles, and hence, additional washing cycles with more pressure were required to remove mustard. After the application of washing cycles, final rinsing with water, and drying with compressed air, the degree of stain removal, erosion, as well as any changes in gloss and colour were evaluated. As one can see from Fig. 11 , neither of the used chemical products left any stains on both control and CDQs-containing coatings nor changed the visual appearance of the coatings.

In this study, it is reported a polyurethane-based matrix with improved UV-shielding properties by incorporating nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (CQDs) as UV absorbers. Different amounts of CQDs were successfully incorporated; in the most promising results were observed at a concentration of 30 mg/gPU (3 wt%), with considerably reduced transmittance in the UVA and UVB range. Notably, the CQDs incorporation into the polyurethane-based matrix did not compromise the physiochemical properties of the commercial coating. The prepared coating exhibited excellent resistance to abrasion, maintaining its integrity even after 500 cycles of abrasive wheels. Additionally, it demonstrated exceptional adhesion to the metallic substrate, ensuring long-lasting performance. Moreover, the coating proved highly washable against various dairy products, showcasing its durability and practicality in real-world applications.

Comparing our findings with previously reported data in the literature, we consider that the observed trend of the UV-shielding properties of the CQDs-containing PU coatings appears to be very promising. Park et al. [29] reported the fabrication of UV and blue-blocking carbon dot films with different carbon dot contents (10, 20, 30, and 40 wt%). Among these formulations, the best-performing film containing 40 wt% of CQDs revealed minimum UV transmittance values of just 20% within a specific region of the UVA radiation (between 320 and 350 nm), and above that, around 30%, in the whole UVB range.

In another work, Barman et al. [30] demonstrated the preparation of 40-μm-thick PVA-based films containing nitrogen-doped-CQDs with excellent UVA-shielding properties but undesired high UVB transmission (maximum around 65%). After a second layer, resulting in a 75-μm-thick film, the authors managed to reduce by 20% the maximum transmittance value in this region but remained to transmit above 40% of the UVB radiation.

Wang and co-workers [31] described the preparation of acrylic latex/ZnO QDs@SiO2 coatings with 440 μm thickness for UV protection, which, although presents relatively low UV transmittance (»10%) below 300 nm, is completely transparent in all the UVA range. Also, the authors prepared a 48-μm-thick coating, which, in addition to the UVA transparency, transmits over 20% in the UVB range.

In the current study, the best-performing coating achieves an average UV transmission of 15% in the full UVA + UVB range (Fig. 7) with 40 μm of thickness, which outperforms the UV-shielding efficiency of reported studies in the literature. By combining the unique UV-shielding properties of the prepared nitrogen-doped CQDs with the desirable characteristics of the polyurethane-based matrix, we have developed a promising approach for enhancing UV protection in coatings without sacrificing the coating's overall quality and performance.

In this work, nitrogen-doped CQDs were successfully prepared by hydrothermal synthesis using citric acid, poly(vinyl alcohol), and polyethyleneimine. Different loadings of CQDs between 0.5 and 30 mg/gPU were incorporated in a clear polyurethane-based matrix, and the respective films with 40 μm thickness were prepared using an automatic bar coater. A gradual decrease in UV transmittance and an increase in UV-shielding capacity were observed as the concentration of CQDs increased. Coatings with 20 and 30 mg/gPU showed UV-shielding capacities above 95% under a UV lamp and direct sunlight. The chemical and mechanical properties of the best-performing coating were evaluated according to standard tests revealing resistance to abrasion, good washability without visual changes, and high adhesion to the metallic substrate. This study showed the great potential of CQDs to be applied as functional additives to produce UV-resistant films.

Clara Ponte: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation. Lucas C. Grosche: Funding acquisition. Yury V. Kolen'ko: Writing – review & editing. Juliana P.S. Sousa: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Methodology, Funding acquisition.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by Biopaint Project (POCI-01-0247-FEDER-072629).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Full-band UV shielding and highly daylight luminescent silane-functionalized graphene quantum dot nanofluids and their arbitrary polymerized hybrid gel glasses
J. Mater. Chem. C

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Xie, Z.; Du, Q.; Wu, Y.; Hao, X.; Liu, C. Full-band UV shielding and highly daylight luminescent silane-functionalized graphene quantum dot nanofluids and their arbitrary polymerized hybrid gel glasses. Journal of Materials Chemistry C 2016, 4(41), 9879–9886. doi:10.1039/C6TC02035F.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Preventing UV-light damage of light sensitive materials using a highly protective UV-absorbing coating
Chem. Soc. Rev.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Zayat, M.; Garcia-Parejo, P.; Levy, D. Preventing UV-light damage of light sensitive materials using a highly protective UV-absorbing coating. Chemical Society Reviews 2007, 36(8), 1270–1281. doi:10.1039/B608888K.
